message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this research claim about how a top deep-research system scores on DeepResearch Bench.

CLAIM: RACE is a PAIRWISE side-by-side judgment in a single prompt, and the target is ALWAYS article_1 while the human reference is ALWAYS article_2 — there is no position randomization or order counterbalancing.
DETAIL: deepresearch_bench_race.py lines 101-102 pass `article_1=target_article, article_2=reference_article` with no shuffle/swap anywhere. The score prompt puts BOTH full articles in one context and instructs: 'Please strictly evaluate and compare <article_1> and <article_2> based on EACH criterion... 2. Comparative Evaluation: Analyze how the two articles perform on each criterion... 3. Score Separately: score each article on each criterion (0-10 continuous).' Anchors: 0-2 very poor, 2-4 poor, 4-6 average, 6-8 good, 8-10 excellent. Consequence: this is a CONTRAST judgment, not an absolute one. The judge writes a comparative 'analysis' field BEFORE emitting scores for each criterion, so a structurally impoverished article (0 subsections, 0 tables, 0 bullets next to a reference with 24 H3s, 10 tables, 91 bullets) gets that contrast narrated into the analysis text on EVERY criterion — including insight criteria — and drags scores that are nominally about analysis depth. Position is fixed, so it is a constant across all systems (not an exploitable lever, but it means measured deltas aren't position noise).
SYSTEM: RACE-judge-itself
SOURCE GIVEN: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/deepresearch_bench_race.py
STATED CONFIDENCE: high

Go to the source. Check it actually says this. Check it is CURRENT (the benchmark's leaderboard and evaluator both
changed in 2026 — the original paper's numbers are stale; Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge).
Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a primary source. We would rather have five verified facts
than thirty plausible ones.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt: Reproduce verbatim the code around lines 90-115, especially any call passing article_1 / article_2. Is there any shuffling, swapping, randomization, or counterbalancing of article order? Which variable is article_1 (target or reference)? Also list what prompt files it loads and what judge model it uses.

content.content:
# Article Order Analysis

**Code around lines 90-115:**

The relevant section shows:
```
user_prompt = merged_score_prompt.format(
    task_prompt=prompt,
    article_1=target_article,
    article_2=reference_article,
    criteria_list=criteria_list_str 
)
```

**Article Assignment:**
- `article_1` = target model's generated article
- `article_2` = reference article

**No Shuffling or Randomization:**
There is no counterbalancing, swapping, or randomization of article order visible in this code. The assignment remains fixed throughout execution.

**Prompt Files Loaded:**
The script imports scoring prompts from:
- `prompt/score_prompt_zh.py` (Chinese evaluation)
- `prompt/score_prompt_en.py` (English evaluation)

**Judge Model:**
The code instantiates `AIClient()` but doesn't explicitly specify which model it uses. That determination occurs within the `AIClient` class implementation, which isn't shown in the provided content.

input.prompt: Quote verbatim the instructions about: (1) comparative evaluation of article_1 vs article_2, (2) "Score Separately" instructions and the 0-10 scale anchors (0-2, 2-4, 4-6, 6-8, 8-10), (3) the required JSON output fields, in particular whether an "analysis" field is emitted before the scores for each criterion.
List the commit history (dates and messages) for this file. What is the most recent commit date?

content.content:
# Instructions from generate_static_score_prompt

**(1) Comparative Evaluation:**
"Analyze how the two articles perform on each criterion, referencing the content and criterion explanation."

**(2) Score Separately & Scale Anchors:**
"Based on your comparative analysis, score each article on each criterion (0-10 points)."

Scale definitions:
- "0-2 points: Very poor performance. Almost completely fails to meet the criterion requirements."
- "2-4 points: Poor performance. Minimally meets the criterion requirements with significant deficiencies."
- "4-6 points: Average performance. Basically meets the criterion requirements, neither good nor bad."
- "6-8 points: Good performance. Largely meets the criterion requirements with notable strengths."
- "8-10 points: Excellent/outstanding performance. Fully meets or exceeds the criterion requirements."

**(3) Required JSON Output Fields:**
The output_format specifies each criterion evaluation contains:
- "criterion" (the text of the criterion)
- "analysis" (comparative analysis)
- "article_1_score" (0-10 continuous value)
- "article_2_score" (0-10 continuous value)

Yes, an "analysis" field is emitted before scores for each criterion.

# Commit History for deepresearch_bench_race.py

Based on the GitHub page provided, there is only **one commit** visible in the history:

**Commit Date:** June 13, 2025

**Commit Message:** "feat: initial release of DeepResearch Bench v1.0"

**Commit SHA:** 214741e474a5347ea7f869ab79d27e7c8734ede7

**Most Recent Commit Date:** June 13, 2025

This appears to be the initial commit for this file, marking the first release of the DeepResearch Bench project.

input.prompt: List the most recent commits with dates and messages. Is there any commit in 2026 changing the evaluator, judge model, or RACE prompt?
What judge/eval model name(s) appear here? Quote any model string defaults.

content.content:
# Judge/Eval Models

The code does not define dedicated judge or evaluation models. Instead, it configures two general-purpose LLM models:

**RACE Model** (scoring stage):
- OpenRouter default: `"openai/gpt-5.5"`
- OpenAI default: `"gpt-5.5"`

**FACT Model** (citation extraction):
- OpenRouter default: `"openai/gpt-5.4-mini"`
- OpenAI default: `"gpt-5.4-mini"`

These are used for content processing rather than as explicit evaluators. The RACE model handles scoring with medium reasoning effort, while FACT uses low reasoning effort for citation tasks.

# Recent Commits and 2026 Evaluator Changes

## Most Recent Commits (May 2026)

1. **May 11, 2026**: "README: point to Gemini-2.5 branch for legacy code"
2. **May 11, 2026**: "README: restrict supported providers to OpenAI official + OpenRouter"
3. **May 11, 2026**: "Switch official evaluator to GPT-5.5 (RACE) / GPT-5.4-mini (FACT)"

## Evaluator Model Changes in 2026

Yes, there is a significant commit addressing evaluation changes. On May 11, 2026, a commit modified the judge model setup, switching "official evaluator to GPT-5.5 (RACE) / GPT-5.4-mini (FACT)". This indicates the evaluation framework transitioned to different OpenAI model versions for scoring on the RACE and FACT benchmarks.

Additionally, an April 24, 2026 commit notes a deprecation timeline for prior evaluation infrastructure, documenting "Gemini-2.5-Pro deprecation on June 17, 2026," signaling a migration away from Google's model for evaluation purposes.

input.reasoning:
Verified directly against the primary source on the CURRENT main branch (fetched today), not the paper.

1) Fixed pairwise order — CONFIRMED. `deepresearch_bench_race.py` on main builds the judge prompt with:
   user_prompt = merged_score_prompt.format(task_prompt=prompt, article_1=target_article, article_2=reference_article, criteria_list=criteria_list_str)
   article_1 is always the target model's article, article_2 is always the human reference. No shuffle, swap, random, or counterbalancing appears anywhere in the file. So position is a constant across all evaluated systems — measured deltas are not position noise, and position is not an exploitable lever.

2) Single-context side-by-side contrast judgment — CONFIRMED. `prompt/score_prompt_en.py` (generate_static_score_prompt) puts both full articles in one prompt and instructs "Comparative Evaluation: Analyze how the two articles perform on each criterion..." then "Score Separately: Based on your comparative analysis, score each article on each criterion (0-10 points)."

3) Anchors — CONFIRMED verbatim: 0-2 very poor, 2-4 poor, 4-6 average, 6-8 good, 8-10 excellent/outstanding.

4) Analysis-before-scores — CONFIRMED. The required per-criterion JSON output object is {"criterion", "analysis", "article_1_score", "article_2_score"} — the comparative "analysis" string is emitted before both scores, on every criterion, so any structural contrast the judge narrates in `analysis` conditions the scores that follow (including insight/depth criteria). This is a mechanism-level inference from the schema, not a stated claim of the repo, but the schema ordering itself is confirmed.

CURRENCY CHECK: the repo did change in 2026 — commits on 2026-05-11 "Switch official evaluator to GPT-5.5 (RACE) / GPT-5.4-mini (FACT)" and a README pointer to a legacy Gemini-2.5 branch; utils/api.py now defaults RACE to gpt-5.5 and FACT to gpt-5.4-mini. That change swapped the judge model only. deepresearch_bench_race.py's article_1/article_2 wiring and the RACE score prompt are unchanged on current main, so the claim survives the 2026 evaluator swap. The claim never asserts a judge identity, so no part of it is stale. Caveat for downstream use: any leaderboard numbers or judge-behavior intuitions inherited from the original paper (Gemini-2.5-Pro judge) ARE stale.
input.corrected: Claim is accurate as written. One adjacent correction for context, in case it is used elsewhere: the RACE judge is no longer Gemini-2.5-Pro. Since the 2026-05-11 commit "Switch official evaluator to GPT-5.5 (RACE) / GPT-5.4-mini (FACT)", utils/api.py on main defaults RACE to gpt-5.5 (medium reasoning effort) and FACT to gpt-5.4-mini (low), with supported providers restricted to OpenAI official + OpenRouter; the Gemini-2.5-based code lives on a legacy branch. Source: https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/commits/main and https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench/main/utils/api.py
